scenario: Stellar Bank ingests 3000 transactions per hour in Camunda 8.8 with a message start event keyed by accountId, and the architects must decide what happens when a second transaction message arrives for an accountId that already drives an active instance, given that the new message is published with a time-to-live of 600 seconds.
question: What does Zeebe do with the new message when an instance triggered by the same correlationKey is still active?

options.text: Zeebe correlates the new message immediately and starts a second concurrent process instance alongside the active one
Zeebe correlates the new message only after the BPMN process ID version has been incremented by redeploying the model
Zeebe discards the new message at once without buffering even when its time-to-live exceeds zero on the publish call
Zeebe does not correlate the new message and buffers it until the active instance completes when its time-to-live exceeds zero

a.text: Incorrect. The message start events section states that when an instance triggered by the same correlationKey is active, the message is not correlated and no new instance is created, so a second concurrent instance is not started.

b.text: Incorrect. Redeploying a new process version does not change how an in-flight correlationKey collision is handled; the buffering rule depends on the message time-to-live, not on the process version.

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation explicitly states that the message is buffered when its time-to-live is greater than zero, so discarding it at once contradicts the documented behaviour.

d.text: Correct. The message start events section says the new message is not correlated and is buffered when time-to-live exceeds zero; once the active instance ends, the buffered message starts a new instance of the latest version.

explanation: On a correlationKey collision the engine suppresses correlation for the new message and buffers it if time-to-live is greater than zero, then correlates the buffered message once the active instance completes or terminates.
